lessons.L09:
# Strict Mocks Without False Confidence

**Module**: M05 · Use Mocks, Fakes, and Contracts Intentionally
**Type**: core
**Estimated time**: 14 minutes
**Claim**: C5 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

The research does not reject mocks. It narrows their job. In Python TDD, mocks are most useful when they stand in for a collaborator your application owns, when they are constrained to resemble the real interface, and when they are placed where the code under test actually looks the collaborator up. That combination is what keeps a mock from becoming false confidence [S002](https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.mock.html) [S013](https://nedbatchelder.com/blog/202202/why_your_mock_still_doesnt_work.html).

Python's `unittest.mock` module is flexible to the point of danger. A plain `MagicMock()` accepts any attribute access and any call, so a test can stay green even after the real interface it is supposed to simulate has changed significantly. If you rename a method on the real class, the mock does not know — it simply invents a new `MagicMock` attribute for the new name, and the old assertion never runs. The test passes, the real behavior is broken, and you do not find out until the code hits something real.

Three practices guard against this. First, use `create_autospec()` to lock the mock to the shape of the real object — it will raise `TypeError` if your test calls a method with the wrong signature. Second, patch at the lookup location, not the definition location — if the code under test does `from myapp.services import Notifier`, patch `myapp.module_under_test.Notifier`, not `myapp.services.Notifier`. Third, keep the mock scope narrow: assert one behavioral outcome, not an implementation path through multiple internal calls.

## Why it matters

Bad mocks create a class of bug that is invisible until production. The test is green, the assertion is passing, but the mock is not actually simulating anything real. Teams that use permissive mocks heavily often find that their test suite does not catch regressions introduced by dependency updates or interface refactoring.

TDD's value is that it catches misunderstandings early. An unconstrained mock short-circuits that: it will agree with any misunderstanding. `autospec` restores the feedback loop by making the mock behave like a real object at the signature level.

The equally common failure — patching at the wrong location — is invisible in the same way. The test feels right, the mock gets called, the assertion passes, but the code under test is using the original unpatched version because the patch replaced something in a namespace the code never looks at [S013](https://nedbatchelder.com/blog/202202/why_your_mock_still_doesnt_work.html).

## Example 1 — unconstrained mock vs autospec

The danger with a plain mock:

```python
from unittest.mock import MagicMock

class Ledger:
    def record(self, invoice_id: str, amount_cents: int) -> None:
        ...

# Unconstrained — dangerous
ledger = MagicMock()
# This call has the wrong argument name but the test still passes:
ledger.record(invoice="INV-1", amount=200)   # wrong kwargs
ledger.record.assert_called_once()           # green — but the real call would fail
```

With `create_autospec`, the wrong call raises immediately:

```python
from unittest.mock import create_autospec

ledger = create_autospec(Ledger, instance=True)
# This now raises TypeError: unexpected keyword argument 'invoice'
ledger.record(invoice="INV-1", amount=200)
```

The test fails at the call site with a clear error, rather than silently passing against a wrong call. The fix is to use the correct signature:

```python
ledger = create_autospec(Ledger, instance=True)

service = BillingService(ledger=ledger)
service.record_payment("INV-1", 200)

ledger.record.assert_called_once_with("INV-1", 200)
```

This is a narrow, honest test: it verifies that `BillingService.record_payment` delegates to the owned `Ledger` collaborator with the correct arguments.

## Example 2 — patching at the right location

The classic mistake: patching where something is defined instead of where the code under test sees it.

```python
# myapp/notifier.py
class EmailNotifier:
    def notify(self, address: str) -> None:
        ...

# myapp/onboarding.py
from myapp.notifier import EmailNotifier

def onboard(email: str) -> None:
    n = EmailNotifier()
    n.notify(email)
```

Wrong patch (patches the definition module — code under test already has the original):

```python
# WRONG — patches myapp.notifier.EmailNotifier but onboarding.py already imported it
with patch("myapp.notifier.EmailNotifier") as mock_cls:
    onboard("a@b.com")
    mock_cls.return_value.notify.assert_called_once()  # may still fail silently
```

Correct patch (patches where the code under test looks it up):

```python
# CORRECT — patches the name in the module that uses it
with patch("myapp.onboarding.EmailNotifier") as mock_cls:
    onboard("a@b.com")
    mock_cls.return_value.notify.assert_called_once_with("a@b.com")
```

The rule is: patch in the namespace where the code under test resolves the name, not in the namespace where the name was defined [S013](https://nedbatchelder.com/blog/202202/why_your_mock_still_doesnt_work.html).

## Example 3 — keeping mock scope narrow with monkeypatch

Pytest's `monkeypatch` is ideal for replacing module-level globals or environment state where `patch()` context managers would add noise. The same narrowness principle applies: replace one thing, assert one behavioral outcome.

```python
# config.py
import os

def get_api_key() -> str:
    return os.environ["PAYMENT_API_KEY"]

# test_payment_service.py
def test_service_uses_configured_api_key(monkeypatch):
    monkeypatch.setenv("PAYMENT_API_KEY", "test-key-123")
    service = PaymentService()
    assert service._api_key == "test-key-123"
```

`monkeypatch` automatically restores the original environment after the test ends. No teardown code required, no risk of state leaking into the next test [S008](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/how-to/monkeypatch.html). The mock scope is as narrow as possible: one environment variable, one assertion about the effect on configuration.

## Key points

- `create_autospec()` locks a mock to the real interface — it raises on wrong signatures rather than silently agreeing [S002](https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.mock.html)
- Patch where the code under test looks up the name, not where the name was defined [S013](https://nedbatchelder.com/blog/202202/why_your_mock_still_doesnt_work.html)
- Narrow scope: one collaborator, one behavioral assertion, not a trace of every internal call
- Mocks are most useful for owned collaborators; for external boundaries, higher-fidelity alternatives often give better feedback
- A test that stays green after the real interface changes is not a safety net — it is a liability

## Go deeper

- [S002](https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.mock.html) — `unittest.mock` reference including `autospec`, `spec`, and correct patch usage
- [S013](https://nedbatchelder.com/blog/202202/why_your_mock_still_doesnt_work.html) — common mock failures and why your test can pass while the behavior is broken
- [S008](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/how-to/monkeypatch.html) — `monkeypatch` for narrow environment and state replacement in pytest

---

lessons.L10:
# When to Prefer Verified Fakes or Contract Checks

**Module**: M05 · Use Mocks, Fakes, and Contracts Intentionally
**Type**: debate
**Estimated time**: 16 minutes
**Claim**: C6 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

Strict mocks are a good tool for owned collaborators. But as soon as the boundary crosses into a system your team does not own — an external HTTP API, an event bus managed by another team, a persistence layer with its own semantics — a mock starts making a promise it cannot keep. It can simulate the shape of the external interface as you understood it when you wrote the test, but it cannot tell you whether that understanding is still correct.

The research draws a clear distinction here: the right question is not "mock or not mock?" but "what is the cheapest test that still tells the truth about this boundary?" [S014](https://pythonspeed.com/articles/verified-fakes/). Sometimes the honest answer is a verified fake. Sometimes it is a Pact-style consumer-provider contract check. Sometimes it is a focused integration test. The choice depends on fidelity needs and maintenance cost — and on being precise about which of these three different things you actually mean when you say "test this boundary."

A second nuance from the research: the term "contract testing" is used inconsistently. Some sources use it loosely for any agreed-interface check. Pact uses it more precisely for consumer-provider interaction verification. When planning a suite, name the exact mechanism, not just the label.

## Why it matters

A suite that mocks every external boundary deeply will stay fast and green — right up to the moment when the external service changes in a way the mock did not anticipate. At that point, the tests do not catch the breakage. The first signal is a production failure.

Verified fakes and contract checks address this by introducing a feedback mechanism: the fake or contract is periodically validated against the real system's behavior. Speed is preserved (you are not calling the real system on every test run), but the gap between what the test assumes and what the real system does is regularly closed.

The tradeoff is maintenance cost and setup complexity. Verified fakes require someone to build and update them. Contract tests require tooling (Pact, or an equivalent) and coordination between teams. Those costs are real — which is why the recommendation is not "always use verified fakes," but "prefer them where deep mocks would otherwise silently lie about a boundary you care about."

## Example 1 — verified fake: a hand-written lightweight implementation

A verified fake is an implementation that is much simpler than the real thing but follows the same interface contract and is tested against the real behavior periodically.

```python
# storage.py — owned interface
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from typing import Optional


class DocumentStore(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def save(self, doc_id: str, content: str) -> None:
        ...

    @abstractmethod
    def load(self, doc_id: str) -> Optional[str]:
        ...


# fakes.py — fast in-memory stand-in for tests
class InMemoryDocumentStore(DocumentStore):
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._store: dict[str, str] = {}

    def save(self, doc_id: str, content: str) -> None:
        self._store[doc_id] = content

    def load(self, doc_id: str) -> Optional[str]:
        return self._store.get(doc_id)
```

The "verified" part: the same behavioral tests that exercise the fake also run against the real `S3DocumentStore`. A shared test suite (a contract test class) is parameterised over both implementations:

```python
@pytest.fixture(params=["fake", "real"])
def store(request):
    if request.param == "fake":
        return InMemoryDocumentStore()
    return S3DocumentStore(bucket="test-bucket")

def test_saved_document_is_loadable(store: DocumentStore):
    store.save("doc-1", "hello world")
    assert store.load("doc-1") == "hello world"

def test_missing_document_returns_none(store: DocumentStore):
    assert store.load("nonexistent") is None
```

The real-store tests run in CI but not in every local unit pass (they are slow and require a bucket). The fake tests run everywhere and are fast. If the real store behavior changes — say the S3 client now returns `""` instead of `None` for missing keys — the verified test catches it [S014](https://pythonspeed.com/articles/verified-fakes/).

## Example 2 — consumer-provider contract test with Pact

When your application calls a service owned by another team, a Pact-style test lets you express your consumer expectations and have the provider verify them independently.

```python
# test_notification_consumer.py
from pact import Consumer, Provider, Like

pact = Consumer("BillingService").has_pact_with(Provider("NotificationService"))


def test_billing_can_request_invoice_notification():
    expected_body = Like({"status": "sent", "notification_id": "some-id"})

    (
        pact
        .given("the notification service is running")
        .upon_receiving("a request to send invoice notification INV-42")
        .with_request(
            method="POST",
            path="/notifications",
            body={"type": "invoice", "invoice_id": "INV-42"},
        )
        .will_respond_with(200, body=expected_body)
    )

    with pact:
        adapter = NotificationAdapter(base_url=pact.uri)
        result  = adapter.send_invoice_notification("INV-42")
        assert result.status == "sent"
```

This test runs without the real notification service. It verifies that your adapter correctly formats the request and handles the response. The provider team runs their own Pact verification to confirm they still satisfy these expectations. Both sides learn about incompatible changes before deployment [S017](https://docs.pact.io/getting_started/how_pact_works).

## Example 3 — when to use a focused integration test instead

Sometimes neither a fake nor a contract check is the right tool. When you need to verify a complex query, a database constraint, a cascade delete, or a transaction boundary, a focused integration test against real infrastructure tells the truth more directly than either approach.

```python
# test_invoice_integration.py  (uses the rollback fixture from L08)

def test_cancelled_invoice_cascades_to_line_items(db):
    """Verify that DELETE on invoices cascades to invoice_items at DB level."""
    invoice = Invoice(customer_id=1, status="pending")
    db.add(invoice)
    db.flush()

    item = InvoiceItem(invoice_id=invoice.id, description="Widget", amount_cents=999)
    db.add(item)
    db.flush()

    db.delete(invoice)
    db.flush()

    remaining = db.query(InvoiceItem).filter_by(invoice_id=invoice.id).all()
    assert remaining == [], "cascade delete should remove line items"
```

A mock session would never catch a missing `ON DELETE CASCADE` constraint in the schema. A verified fake would catch it only if the fake implements the same cascade behavior. Only a real database test tells the truth here [S016](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/session_transaction.html#joining-a-session-into-an-external-transaction-such-as-for-test-suites).

The decision rule: use a verified fake or contract check when the cost of calling the real system is too high for the feedback loop you need. Use a real integration test when the database or service semantics themselves are what you are trying to learn.

## Key points

- Deep mocks at external boundaries preserve speed but can silently drift from reality [S014](https://pythonspeed.com/articles/verified-fakes/)
- A verified fake is a lightweight implementation that is periodically validated against the real system — speed plus higher behavioral truth
- Consumer-provider contract tests protect interaction shapes between independently evolving services [S017](https://docs.pact.io/getting_started/how_pact_works)
- "Contract testing" is an overloaded term — define the exact mechanism when planning a suite
- Focused integration tests still have a place for database semantics, constraints, and transaction behavior that no fake can accurately model [S016](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/session_transaction.html#joining-a-session-into-an-external-transaction-such-as-for-test-suites)

## Go deeper

- [S014](https://pythonspeed.com/articles/verified-fakes/) — verified fakes: a higher-assurance alternative to ordinary hand-rolled test doubles
- [S017](https://docs.pact.io/getting_started/how_pact_works) — how Pact implements consumer-provider contract verification
- [S016](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/session_transaction.html#joining-a-session-into-an-external-transaction-such-as-for-test-suites) — transaction rollback strategy and why real database tests are sometimes the only honest choice

# Parametrization and Property-Based Testing

**Module**: M06 · Broaden Specification Beyond Single Examples
**Type**: advanced
**Estimated time**: 15 minutes
**Claim**: C7 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

Single examples are where most TDD work starts, and for good reason: they are readable, concrete, and fast to write. But they have a ceiling. Once a behavior has more than two or three interesting input families, writing one example at a time stops being the most efficient way to specify it. And for behaviors with very large or continuous input spaces, hand-picking examples may leave whole regions of the behavior unexplored without you realizing it.

Python TDD provides two tools for moving past that ceiling. Parametrization lets you express a known family of cases in one test design — the same assertion, applied systematically across a table of named examples [S005](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/how-to/parametrize.html). Property-based testing with Hypothesis takes the next step: you define an invariant that should hold across a broad class of inputs, then the library generates inputs and searches for counterexamples you did not think to write [S010](https://hypothesis.readthedocs.io/en/latest/quickstart.html).

The two tools answer different questions. Parametrization says: "This behavior should produce exactly these outputs for these named cases." Hypothesis says: "This property should hold for any input the library can generate — find the smallest one that breaks it." Neither replaces small example-driven TDD. They extend it when the example space becomes too large or unpredictable to express manually.

## Why it matters

A test suite with one happy-path case, one failure case, and one edge case per function is often under-specified in ways that are hard to see by reading the tests. The tests are green, but the behavior on inputs you did not explicitly choose is undefined territory. Bugs from that territory are often the hardest to reproduce and diagnose, because the test suite gave no signal that the input space had interesting structure beyond the three hand-picked cases.

Parametrization and property-based testing both sharpen specification coverage. Parametrization makes a case family explicit — the developer chooses the cases intentionally and names them. Hypothesis explores beyond the developer's choices, looking for inputs where the stated invariant breaks. Both changes reduce the gap between what the tests verify and what the function is actually expected to do.

## Example 1 — starting point: three separate examples that want to be parametrized

An initial pass through `normalize_tag` might produce three separate tests:

```python
def test_strips_whitespace():
    assert normalize_tag("  Python  ") == "python"

def test_lowercases():
    assert normalize_tag("PYTHON") == "python"

def test_blank_becomes_none():
    assert normalize_tag("   ") is None
```

Each test is simple, but they share an identical structure: call `normalize_tag(input)`, assert the output. That repetition is the signal to consolidate with `pytest.mark.parametrize`:

```python
import pytest


@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    ("raw", "expected"),
    [
        ("  Python  ", "python"),     # trims and lowercases
        ("PYTHON",     "python"),     # only lowercases
        ("python",     "python"),     # already normal
        ("   ",        None),         # blank → None
        ("",           None),         # empty → None
        ("C++",        "c++"),        # non-alpha characters preserved
    ],
)
def test_normalize_tag(raw: str, expected: str | None) -> None:
    assert normalize_tag(raw) == expected
```

Now six cases live in one test. Adding a seventh is one line in the table. Pytest reports each case individually if it fails, so you still get precise diagnostics. The parametrized form is more expressive, not less readable [S005](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/how-to/parametrize.html).

## Example 2 — property-based testing with Hypothesis

After establishing the specific cases, you may want to state a broader invariant. For `normalize_tag`, one natural invariant is idempotency: normalizing an already-normalized tag should return the same tag. Another is the output type: the result is always either a lowercase string or `None`.

```python
from hypothesis import given, strategies as st


@given(st.text())
def test_normalize_tag_is_idempotent(raw: str) -> None:
    """Normalizing twice should give the same result as normalizing once."""
    once  = normalize_tag(raw)
    twice = normalize_tag(raw) if once is None else normalize_tag(once)
    assert once == twice


@given(st.text())
def test_normalize_tag_result_type(raw: str) -> None:
    """Result is always str (lowercase) or None — never anything else."""
    result = normalize_tag(raw)
    assert result is None or (isinstance(result, str) and result == result.lower())
```

Hypothesis generates hundreds of inputs across runs, then shrinks any falsifying example to its minimum form. If your implementation has a bug that only appears with a Unicode character, a zero-width space, or a very specific combination of whitespace, Hypothesis will find it and show you the smallest input that demonstrates the failure [S010](https://hypothesis.readthedocs.io/en/latest/quickstart.html).

## Example 3 — applying both techniques to a business function

A discount calculation rule: orders over a threshold earn a percentage discount. The discount rate should never exceed 1.0, and orders below the threshold should always receive 0 discount.

Specific cases with parametrization:

```python
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    ("total_cents", "threshold_cents", "rate", "expected_discount_cents"),
    [
        (10_000, 5_000, 0.10, 1_000),   # above threshold → discount applied
        (5_000,  5_000, 0.10, 0),        # exactly at threshold → no discount (exclusive)
        (4_999,  5_000, 0.10, 0),        # below threshold → no discount
        (10_000, 5_000, 0.00, 0),        # zero rate → no discount
    ],
)
def test_calculate_discount(total_cents, threshold_cents, rate, expected_discount_cents):
    assert calculate_discount(total_cents, threshold_cents, rate) == expected_discount_cents
```

Invariant with Hypothesis: the discount should never exceed the total order amount, and should always be non-negative:

```python
from hypothesis import given, settings, strategies as st


@given(
    total_cents    = st.integers(min_value=0, max_value=1_000_000),
    threshold_cents = st.integers(min_value=1, max_value=1_000_000),
    rate           = st.floats(min_value=0.0, max_value=1.0, allow_nan=False),
)
def test_discount_is_non_negative_and_bounded(total_cents, threshold_cents, rate):
    discount = calculate_discount(total_cents, threshold_cents, rate)
    assert 0 <= discount <= total_cents
```

The parametrized tests verify named cases precisely. The Hypothesis test guards against a whole class of implementation errors — negative discounts, discounts larger than the order, off-by-one threshold bugs — across the full input space. Together they give stronger behavioral coverage than either technique alone.

## Key points

- Parametrize when you have a known family of named cases that share a test structure [S005](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/how-to/parametrize.html)
- Use Hypothesis when you can state a meaningful invariant and want the library to search for counterexamples [S010](https://hypothesis.readthedocs.io/en/latest/quickstart.html)
- Both techniques extend small example-driven TDD rather than replace it
- Hypothesis output is a falsifying example shrunk to its minimum — if it fails, the error is always reproducible and minimal
- Avoid large parametrized matrices with no clear pattern — if the data does not tell a story, the test probably covers too much or too little

## Go deeper

- [S005](https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/how-to/parametrize.html) — parametrize usage including IDs, indirect fixtures, and stacking multiple decorators
- [S010](https://hypothesis.readthedocs.io/en/latest/quickstart.html) — Hypothesis quickstart: strategies, given decorator, settings, and shrinking behavior
- [S007](https://tidyfirst.substack.com/p/canon-tdd) — how broader specification fits into the TDD loop without abandoning the example-first discipline

lessons.L12:
# Advanced Boundary Coverage in Practice

**Module**: M06 · Broaden Specification Beyond Single Examples
**Type**: synthesis
**Estimated time**: 17 minutes
**Claim**: C7 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

The previous five lessons built separate ideas: fixture isolation, async loop control, transactional persistence testing, seam design, strict mocks, verified fakes, contract checks, parametrization, and property-based testing. The synthesis challenge is knowing when to apply each. A practitioner who reaches for Hypothesis on every function is doing unnecessary work. One who only writes happy-path unit tests is leaving real coverage gaps. Advanced coverage is about deliberate layering, not exhaustive application of every technique [S015](https://pytest-asyncio.readthedocs.io/en/stable/concepts.html) [S016](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/session_transaction.html#joining-a-session-into-an-external-transaction-such-as-for-test-suites).

The research suggests a practical framing: choose each test type by the kind of learning it provides. Unit tests with strict mocks tell you whether domain logic makes correct decisions. Parametrized and property-based tests tell you whether the specification holds across a broader input space. Async loop or transaction fixture tests tell you whether runtime lifecycle boundaries preserve isolation. Real boundary tests — integration, contract, or verified-fake — tell you whether translations to external systems are still honest. Each layer earns its place by answering a different question about the system.

## Why it matters

Advanced coverage is not about having more tests. It is about having the right tests for the right concerns. A suite that overlaps heavily — unit tests, integration tests, and contract tests all covering the same assertion — is expensive to maintain and provides diminishing feedback. A suite with strategic layering provides fast feedback on domain decisions and honest feedback on boundary translations, at a sustainable maintenance cost.

The other risk is coverage theatre: a suite that is large and green but doesn't actually catch the regressions it implies it will catch. Parametrization applied to the wrong granularity, Hypothesis invariants that are too easy to satisfy, mocks that don't resemble the real interfaces — all of these produce test count without test value. The advanced skill is not running every tool, but knowing which tool answers the question you actually have.

## Example 1 — a complete suite for a domain service with boundaries

A `SubscriptionRenewalService` that renews subscriptions: validate eligibility, charge via a payment gateway, update the database, emit a domain event.

The ideal suite has distinct layers, each with a clear purpose:

```python
# Layer 1 — unit tests for domain logic (strict mocks, fast)
def test_ineligible_subscription_is_rejected(mock_gateway, mock_repo, mock_bus):
    repo  = create_autospec(SubscriptionRepo, instance=True)
    sub   = Subscription(id="sub-1", status="cancelled", expires_at=past_date())
    repo.find.return_value = sub

    service = SubscriptionRenewalService(repo, mock_gateway, mock_bus)
    with pytest.raises(IneligibleSubscriptionError):
        service.renew("sub-1")

    mock_gateway.charge.assert_not_called()
    mock_bus.publish.assert_not_called()


def test_successful_renewal_emits_event():
    repo    = create_autospec(SubscriptionRepo, instance=True)
    gateway = create_autospec(PaymentGateway, instance=True)
    bus     = create_autospec(EventBus, instance=True)

    sub = Subscription(id="sub-1", status="active", expires_at=tomorrow())
    repo.find.return_value = sub
    gateway.charge.return_value = ChargeResult(status="ok", charge_id="ch-99")

    service = SubscriptionRenewalService(repo, gateway, bus)
    service.renew("sub-1")

    bus.publish.assert_called_once_with(SubscriptionRenewed(subscription_id="sub-1"))
```

```python
# Layer 2 — parametrized cases for eligibility rules
@pytest.mark.parametrize(
    ("status", "days_until_expiry", "should_renew"),
    [
        ("active",    5,    True),
        ("active",    0,    True),
        ("cancelled", 5,    False),
        ("suspended", 5,    False),
        ("active",   -1,    False),   # already expired
    ],
)
def test_renewal_eligibility(status, days_until_expiry, should_renew):
    sub = Subscription(
        id="sub-1",
        status=status,
        expires_at=date.today() + timedelta(days=days_until_expiry),
    )
    assert sub.is_eligible_for_renewal() == should_renew
```

```python
# Layer 3 — real database test for persistence (transaction fixture)
def test_renewal_updates_expiry_in_database(db):
    sub = Subscription(id="sub-1", status="active", expires_at=tomorrow())
    db.add(sub)
    db.flush()

    repo    = SQLAlchemySubscriptionRepo(db)
    gateway = create_autospec(PaymentGateway, instance=True)
    gateway.charge.return_value = ChargeResult(status="ok", charge_id="ch-1")
    bus     = create_autospec(EventBus, instance=True)

    service = SubscriptionRenewalService(repo, gateway, bus)
    service.renew("sub-1")

    refreshed = db.query(Subscription).filter_by(id="sub-1").one()
    assert refreshed.expires_at > tomorrow()   # expiry was extended
```

Each layer is doing different work. The unit tests verify logic. The parametrized cases cover the eligibility space. The database test verifies that the repository correctly persists the renewal outcome under real session semantics [S016](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/session_transaction.html#joining-a-session-into-an-external-transaction-such-as-for-test-suites).

## Example 2 — adding a Hypothesis invariant

Once the unit tests and parametrized cases are in place, a property-based test can guard a broader invariant across the renewal logic: a renewal should never produce an expiry date in the past, regardless of the input state.

```python
from hypothesis import given, strategies as st, assume


@given(
    status         = st.sampled_from(["active"]),
    days_remaining = st.integers(min_value=0, max_value=365),
    renewal_days   = st.integers(min_value=1, max_value=365),
)
def test_renewal_always_extends_expiry_into_future(status, days_remaining, renewal_days):
    sub = Subscription(
        id="sub-1",
        status=status,
        expires_at=date.today() + timedelta(days=days_remaining),
    )
    new_expiry = sub.calculate_renewal_expiry(renewal_days)
    assert new_expiry > date.today()
```

This test does not replace the specific parametrized cases. It adds a different kind of assurance: that across any combination of remaining days and renewal period, the extension logic never produces a nonsensical past date [S010](https://hypothesis.readthedocs.io/en/latest/quickstart.html).

## Example 3 — async layer with per-test loop scope

If the service has an async notification path — say, an HTTP call to a webhook endpoint — the suite extends into async territory with an explicit per-test event loop to keep tests independent [S015](https://pytest-asyncio.readthedocs.io/en/stable/concepts.html).

```python
import pytest
import pytest_asyncio


@pytest_asyncio.fixture()
async def async_notifier():
    """Fresh async notifier per test — no shared event loop state."""
    notifier = AsyncWebhookNotifier(endpoint="http://localhost:9999/hook")
    yield notifier
    await notifier.close()


@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_renewal_webhook_fires_on_success(async_notifier, httpx_mock):
    httpx_mock.add_response(url="http://localhost:9999/hook", status_code=200)

    await async_notifier.notify_renewal("sub-1")

    requests = httpx_mock.get_requests()
    assert len(requests) == 1
    assert requests[0].method == "POST"
```

The per-test fixture scope ensures each async test starts with a fresh loop. The mock HTTP response keeps the test fast and isolated. Real integration-level checks against the actual webhook endpoint run in a separate CI stage, not on every local test run.

## Decision model

| Concern | Tool |
|---|---|
| Domain logic decisions | Unit test with strict mock (autospec) |
| Input case families | Parametrized test |
| Broad invariants | Hypothesis property test |
| Real persistence semantics | Transaction-wrapped integration test |
| External service interaction shape | Contract test or verified fake |
| Async runtime isolation | Per-test event loop scope |

## Key points

- Advanced coverage is strategic layering, not exhaustive application of every tool [S016](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/session_transaction.html#joining-a-session-into-an-external-transaction-such-as-for-test-suites)
- Each layer earns its place by answering a question the other layers cannot answer
- Unit tests protect logic; parametrization broadens cases; Hypothesis guards invariants; integration tests verify boundary truth [S010](https://hypothesis.readthedocs.io/en/latest/quickstart.html)
- Per-test async event loop scope prevents lifecycle state from leaking across test runs [S015](https://pytest-asyncio.readthedocs.io/en/stable/concepts.html)
- The right suite for a non-trivial Python system is chosen by learning goals, not by attachment to one test style

## Go deeper

- [S015](https://pytest-asyncio.readthedocs.io/en/stable/concepts.html) — async loop scope, per-test vs session-scope event loops
- [S016](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/orm/session_transaction.html#joining-a-session-into-an-external-transaction-such-as-for-test-suites) — transactional test isolation for realistic persistence tests
- [S010](https://hypothesis.readthedocs.io/en/latest/quickstart.html) — Hypothesis in context: combining with example-based tests rather than replacing them

---

# What the Empirical Evidence Actually Supports

**Module**: M07 · Adopt TDD Without Overclaiming It
**Type**: debate
**Estimated time**: 14 minutes
**Claim**: C8 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

TDD attracts unusually strong opinions for a software practice. Advocates describe it as a near-universal improvement in code quality, design clarity, and long-term maintainability. Skeptics treat mixed experimental results as evidence that TDD is primarily an ideology with a sophisticated vocabulary. The research supports neither position. After examining two peer-reviewed outcome studies and the broader practitioner evidence base, the clearest finding is narrower and more honest: the empirical evidence on TDD outcomes is mixed, and the effects appear highly sensitive to context, team skill, task complexity, and what outcome is being measured [S018](https://cris.vtt.fi/en/publications/an-industry-experiment-on-the-effects-of-test-driven-development-/) [S019](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10664-020-09895-8).

This is not a weak conclusion. It is a precise one. "The evidence is mixed and context-dependent" is a fundamentally different statement from "there is no evidence." The evidence exists — some studies show quality benefits, others show task-dependent productivity tradeoffs, and the pattern across experiments is that smaller, more controlled tasks tend to produce the most favorable results for TDD, while more complex, realistic tasks produce more heterogeneous outcomes.

## The two studies in depth

**Tosun et al. (2017)** was an industry experiment in which professional developers were randomly assigned to TDD or test-last development. The study found no statistically significant difference in code quality between the two groups, and productivity effects were task-sensitive: TDD was associated with slower completion on tasks that participants were less familiar with, and showed no consistent advantage on quality metrics across the sample [S018](https://cris.vtt.fi/en/publications/an-industry-experiment-on-the-effects-of-test-driven-development-/).

**Santos et al. (2021)** conducted a family-of-experiments meta-analysis aggregating multiple smaller controlled studies. One notable finding: novice programmers slightly favored iterative test-last development on quality outcomes in some contexts, and task characteristics — not the TDD treatment itself — were the dominant factor in predicting outcomes [S019](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10664-020-09895-8).

What both studies share is this: the headline claims that are commonly made about TDD — "it reliably improves quality," "it consistently improves productivity," "it reduces defect rates by X%" — are not supported by the controlled experimental evidence at a level that would justify treating them as universal.

## Why the evidence looks like this

There are several reasons the experimental picture is messier than TDD advocates often imply.

First, the practice of TDD varies enormously between teams. A team that writes one assertion-heavy test per function, skips refactoring, uses loose mocks throughout, and calls the result "TDD" is not doing the same thing as a team that drives design from a test list, maintains tight isolation, and treats refactoring as a first-class activity. Outcome studies often cannot control for practice quality.

Second, TDD's clearest benefits are at the design and feedback level, not purely at the defect level. Fowler argues that the value of TDD is in its effect on design decisions — forcing earlier thinking about interfaces, raising the cost of bad coupling, keeping code change-ready [S006](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/TestDrivenDevelopment.html). Those benefits are real but hard to capture in a short controlled experiment.

Third, the studies that exist tend to use student or novice participants, constrained tasks, and academic settings. Generalizing those results to expert practitioners on production systems of realistic complexity is a significant extrapolation.

## What you should actually do with this

First, stop quoting blanket outcome statistics to justify TDD adoption. The evidence does not support that level of confidence.

Second, do not use mixed evidence as a reason to skip the practice. Mixed experimental results are consistent with TDD having real design and feedback value while the quality and productivity effects being context-dependent. The practice is worth evaluating locally on your own codebase and team.

Third, measure what you can when you adopt TDD. Track defect rates, change failure frequency, refactoring confidence, and test maintenance cost over a meaningful period. Use your own data to build a local evidence base rather than importing claims from someone else's.

Fourth, distinguish practice quality from practice label. If the team calls itself TDD but skips refactoring, uses unconstrained mocks, and never revisits the test list discipline — the outcome studies about properly-practiced TDD are not relevant to that team's situation anyway.

## Key points

- The empirical evidence on TDD outcomes is mixed and context-sensitive — not empty, but not uniformly positive [S018](https://cris.vtt.fi/en/publications/an-industry-experiment-on-the-effects-of-test-driven-development-/) [S019](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10664-020-09895-8)
- Blanket advocacy ("TDD always improves quality") is not supported by controlled experimental evidence
- Blanket rejection ("TDD has no value") ignores real design and feedback benefits documented in practitioner literature [S006](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/TestDrivenDevelopment.html)
- Task characteristics, team skill, and practice quality are more powerful predictors of outcome than the TDD/TLD label alone
- The right stance is local evaluation with real metrics, not global conviction based on someone else's experiment

## Go deeper

- [S018](https://cris.vtt.fi/en/publications/an-industry-experiment-on-the-effects-of-test-driven-development-/) — the Tosun industry experiment: professional developers, random assignment, no significant quality difference
- [S019](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10664-020-09895-8) — Santos meta-analysis: aggregated experiments showing task-driven effects and context sensitivity
- [S006](https://martinfowler.com/bliki/TestDrivenDevelopment.html) — Fowler on the design-level value of TDD that controlled quality experiments may not fully capture

---

lessons.L14:
# Open Limits: Large Systems, Contract Ambiguity, and Next Evidence

**Module**: M07 · Adopt TDD Without Overclaiming It
**Type**: gap
**Estimated time**: 13 minutes
**Claim**: C6, C8 from Strata synthesis

---

## The core idea

A course that ends by pretending it answered every question teaches false confidence. The research behind this course is strong: 19 active sources, 8 claims, 6 research questions answered. But it also has three real limits that stayed open even after the empirical refresh pass. This final lesson names them explicitly. Understanding where the evidence thins out is part of what it means to apply this knowledge responsibly.

The three open limits are:

**Large-system evidence is thin.** Most of what we can say confidently about Python TDD comes from framework documentation, expert practitioner writing, and peer-reviewed experiments on constrained tasks with controlled participants. That is a solid foundation for small-to-medium Python services and libraries. It is not enough to make strong predictions about how the same patterns perform in very large systems with dozens of services, layered organizational constraints, mixed technical debt, and evolving compliance requirements.

**"Contract testing" is still ambiguous.** The source set uses this term in at least two distinct senses that are easy to conflate. Some sources mean a consumer-side test of expected interaction shapes (the Pact meaning). Others use it loosely to mean any kind of realistic interface compatibility check. If your team adopts "contract testing," you need to resolve which mechanism you actually mean before making adoption decisions.

**The empirical evidence is mixed and non-transferable.** The two outcome studies in the research set are real and informative, but they come with important caveats: neither is Python-specific, neither captures the design-level benefits that practitioner sources emphasize, and neither provides guidance on how outcomes might scale with team experience, codebase age, or domain complexity. Transferring their results to your context requires caution.

## Why naming gaps matters

The instinct when teaching is to smooth over uncertainty. It is easier to present confident rules than to hand learners something incomplete. But in engineering, knowing the confidence level of a belief is as important as knowing the belief itself. A practitioner who applies a rule with calibrated uncertainty will observe carefully, gather local evidence, and update their approach. One who applies it with false certainty will explain away disconfirming signals until the system fails.

The gaps in this research are not failures of the project. They are the right endpoint. They tell you where the evidence is strong, where it is reasonable, and where you are in genuinely uncertain territory that your own practice needs to help resolve.

## Gap 1 in practice: operating at larger scale

If you are working in a system with five or more services, significant organizational constraints, or a Python codebase that has grown through multiple technology generations, the lessons in this course give you the right principles — seam design, isolation, boundary coverage, parametrization — but the calibration of how much to invest in each layer, and which layers provide the most return, will require your own empirical work.

Operating constraints for large-system TDD adoption:
- Validate suite design incrementally. Do not assume that a strategy that works for a 3-service system scales unchanged to a 20-service system.
- Track maintenance cost per test layer, not just coverage percentage. A layer that is expensive to maintain relative to the bugs it catches may need to be consolidated.
- Be explicit about where mocking at scale creates synchronization risk — service mocks that fall behind real service behavior are a large-system problem that small-system TDD discipline alone does not prevent.

## Gap 2 in practice: resolving "contract testing" before adopting it

Before committing to "contract testing" as a team practice, answer:

1. **Whose expectations are being verified?** Consumer-driven (Pact) means the consuming service defines the expected interaction and the provider verifies it. Provider-driven means the provider defines the contract and consumers validate against it. These are different tools for different coordination problems.

2. **Where do the contracts live?** In a Pact broker, in a shared repository, in recorded HTTP fixtures, or somewhere else? The answer affects how contract updates propagate across teams.

3. **How are mismatches handled?** When a provider changes an interaction and breaks a consumer contract, what is the process for coordinating the update? Without this, contract tests become a source of friction rather than confidence.

Defining these three points before adoption turns a vague label into an actionable mechanism. Without them, "we do contract testing" may mean something different to every team member.

## Gap 3 in practice: building your own evidence base

Given that the experimental evidence is mixed and not Python-specific, the most defensible approach to TDD outcome claims in your organization is local measurement over time.

A minimal evidence-gathering approach:
- Before full adoption, baseline your current defect rate, change failure frequency, and average time to reproduce production bugs
- Adopt TDD practices in one team or one service for a defined period (six to twelve months minimum — shorter periods are too noisy)
- After the period, compare the same metrics against the baseline and against teams or services that did not change their approach
- Report the comparison with uncertainty bounds, not as a headline figure

This is slower than citing a study, and the sample size will be small. But it produces evidence that is directly relevant to your environment, your team, and your codebase — which is more useful than a controlled study performed on students in a laboratory.

## Key points

- Large-system case studies are thinner in the research base than small-service technique documentation — calibrate accordingly
- "Contract testing" is an overloaded term: define the exact mechanism (consumer-driven, provider-driven, recorded fixtures) before adopting it [S017](https://docs.pact.io/getting_started/how_pact_works)
- The empirical outcome studies are mixed, non-Python-specific, and do not transfer cleanly to all contexts [S018](https://cris.vtt.fi/en/publications/an-industry-experiment-on-the-effects-of-test-driven-development-/) [S019](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10664-020-09895-8)
- Build a local evidence base with explicit baselines rather than relying on transferring external results
- Honest adoption means turning open gaps into explicit operating constraints and measurement plans, not ignoring them

## Go deeper

- [S017](https://docs.pact.io/getting_started/how_pact_works) — how Pact defines consumer-driven contract testing precisely — useful for resolving the terminology gap
- [S018](https://cris.vtt.fi/en/publications/an-industry-experiment-on-the-effects-of-test-driven-development-/) — the industry experiment that illustrates why outcome results are task-sensitive and do not transfer cleanly
- [S019](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10664-020-09895-8) — the meta-analysis showing context sensitivity and why "TDD is better" is not the finding

---

cards.front: What makes canonical TDD more than just writing tests before code?
cards.back: Canonical TDD is a behavior-design loop: write the next failing behavior check, make it pass with the smallest useful change, and refactor so the design stays easy to extend.

cards.front: What is the difference between 'test first' and behavior-first TDD?
cards.back: 'Test first' can still mean confirming a design you already invented. Behavior-first TDD uses each test to expose the next needed behavior so the interface and structure emerge incrementally.

cards.front: What is the best sign that your next TDD step is too large?
cards.back: If the next test forces you to imagine several future abstractions before you can write it, the step is too large. Pick a smaller observable behavior instead.

cards.front: What question should you ask after a test turns green to decide whether to refactor now?
cards.back: Ask whether the latest change made the next likely behavior harder to express or extend cleanly. If it did, refactor before continuing.

cards.front: What is the difference between 'green' and 'good enough to move on' in TDD?
cards.back: Green only means the current behavior passes. Good enough to move on means the code is still readable and the next test can be added without obvious design strain.

cards.front: Why is small-step control central to refactoring judgment?
cards.back: Small steps let you refactor only when current evidence justifies it. That avoids both speculative redesign and brittle code growth with no cleanup.

cards.front: Why is test isolation a first-order concern in Python TDD?
cards.back: Because the red-green-refactor loop only stays trustworthy when each test controls its own environment. Shared hidden state can make passing and failing signals lie.

cards.front: What fixture scope should you usually default to when you want the most reliable TDD feedback?
cards.back: Function scope. It gives the strongest default isolation and reduces the chance that tests start depending on hidden order or stale shared state.

cards.front: What is the difference between convenient setup and trustworthy cleanup?
cards.back: Convenient setup only prepares state. Trustworthy cleanup guarantees that each test leaves no hidden residue for the next one.

cards.front: Why do async tests usually still run sequentially in a reliable Python test suite?
cards.back: Because the priority is controlled, isolated runtime behavior. Sequential execution avoids hidden event-loop interactions that can make async tests flaky or misleading.

cards.front: What problem does wrapping a database test in an outer transaction solve?
cards.back: It lets application code behave realistically, including commits, while still guaranteeing the test's changes are rolled back before the next test runs.

cards.front: What is the difference between realistic boundary testing and mocking everything away?
cards.back: Realistic boundary testing preserves the behavior you need to learn from while controlling lifecycle around it. Mocking everything often removes the very boundary truth you needed.

cards.front: Why is pytest the pragmatic default for modern Python TDD?
cards.back: Because it lowers friction in the feedback loop with plain test functions, fixtures, parametrization, and clearer failures, making the next useful test easier to write.

cards.front: When does parametrization start adding more value than duplicating separate tests?
cards.back: When several cases express the same behavior pattern and you want broader coverage without rewriting the test body each time.

cards.front: What is the difference between pytest being nicer and pytest being strategically useful for TDD?
cards.back: Being nicer is about taste. Being strategically useful means it reduces enough friction that developers can keep working in short, behavior-by-behavior cycles.

cards.front: What is the safest way to start improving a unittest-heavy codebase with pytest?
cards.back: Start by running the existing suite with pytest for better execution ergonomics, then migrate tests into pytest-native structure only where the extra features reduce real friction.

cards.front: What does running unittest tests through pytest not automatically give you?
cards.back: It does not automatically give `TestCase` methods full pytest fixture argument injection or pytest-style parametrization. Structural limits still remain.

cards.front: What rule should guide migration from unittest to pytest-native style?
cards.back: Migrate by pressure, not ideology. Rewrite the parts where fixtures, parametrization, or simpler structure materially improve the suite.

cards.front: What is an owned seam in Python test design?
cards.back: It is a boundary where your domain logic depends on concepts your application owns rather than on the call shapes or object models of third-party systems.

cards.front: Why does dependency injection usually make business tests clearer?
cards.back: Because the test can provide a collaborator directly instead of patching a third-party client constructed deep inside the business code.

cards.front: What is the difference between an adapter and direct vendor coupling?
cards.back: An adapter translates from the vendor's surface into your application's own interface. Direct coupling makes business code and tests speak the vendor's language directly.

cards.front: Where is a seam especially valuable in a real service boundary?
cards.back: At the point where foreign transport or persistence detail becomes domain intent, such as turning raw API calls into business actions like `charge` or `save invoice`.

cards.front: What is the difference between a domain test and a boundary-verification test?
cards.back: A domain test checks business decisions through owned collaborators. A boundary-verification test checks whether the adapter or translation layer still speaks correctly to the real system.

cards.front: Why does introducing a seam not remove the need for higher-fidelity tests?
cards.back: Because the translation layer itself can still be wrong. The seam simplifies domain tests, but the adapter or persistence boundary still needs realistic verification.

cards.front: What does `autospec` protect you from when using mocks?
cards.back: It constrains the mock to the real interface or callable shape, reducing the risk that the test stays green against a collaborator API that no longer exists.

cards.front: Where should you usually patch a collaborator in Python tests?
cards.back: Patch it where the code under test looks it up, not merely where the object was originally defined.

cards.front: What is the difference between a narrow collaborator mock and a deep external mock?
cards.back: A narrow collaborator mock stands in for an owned business-facing interface. A deep external mock reproduces third-party transport detail and tends to become brittle and overfitted.

cards.front: What is the difference between a verified fake and a deep mock of an external service?
cards.back: A verified fake is a simplified stand-in whose behavior is checked against the real service. A deep mock only mirrors your current assumptions about the service interface.

cards.front: When are contract-style checks especially useful?
cards.back: When independently evolving services need explicit verification that their interaction expectations still match on both consumer and provider sides.

cards.front: What question helps you choose between mocks, fakes, contract checks, and integration tests?
cards.back: Ask for the cheapest test that still tells the truth about the boundary you care about.

cards.front: When is parametrization the right way to broaden a TDD specification?
cards.back: When you already understand a family of concrete cases and want to express them clearly without duplicating the test body.

cards.front: What does a Hypothesis falsifying example give you that hand-written examples often do not?
cards.back: It gives you a concrete counterexample the generated property discovered, often smaller and more surprising than the cases you would have written by hand.

cards.front: What is the difference between an example and a property in test design?
cards.back: An example states one concrete case. A property states an invariant that should hold across many possible inputs.

cards.front: What principle should determine which advanced test layers you add to a Python suite?
cards.back: Choose layers by the kind of truth you need to learn: domain logic, broader invariants, lifecycle behavior, or boundary translation correctness.

cards.front: Why might one component legitimately use unit tests, a transaction-backed integration test, and a contract check together?
cards.back: Because each test answers a different question: business logic, realistic persistence behavior, and correctness of the external interaction boundary.

cards.front: What is the difference between deliberate layered coverage and using every test type by habit?
cards.back: Deliberate layering assigns each test type a specific learning job. Habitual accumulation adds overlapping tests without a clear reason for each layer.

cards.front: What is the difference between mixed evidence and evidence of no value?
cards.back: Mixed evidence means outcomes vary by context and execution quality. It does not mean the practice is worthless or disproven in every setting.

cards.front: What should a team measure locally instead of assuming TDD outcomes in advance?
cards.back: Measures like defect rates, change confidence, maintenance burden, and delivery speed in the team's own environment rather than relying on universal claims.

cards.front: What is the safest summary of the empirical TDD literature in this course?
cards.back: TDD may improve outcomes in some contexts, but the evidence is mixed and context-sensitive enough that teams should validate effects locally.

cards.front: What does it mean to treat a research gap as an operating constraint?
cards.back: It means you do not hide the uncertainty. You turn it into an explicit limit on what you claim and a prompt for local validation.

cards.front: What should a team do before saying it uses 'contract tests'?
cards.back: Define the exact mechanism. The label can mean Pact-style consumer-provider checks, broader interface compatibility tests, or something looser.

cards.front: Why are large-system limits part of honest TDD adoption rather than a side note?
cards.back: Because evidence and guidance from smaller or narrower settings do not automatically scale. Large-system use needs explicit validation instead of borrowed certainty.

M02.title: Control State, Isolation, and Cleanup - Quick Check

questions.question: Why does weak isolation damage TDD twice over?
questions.answer: It damages both the reliability of test feedback and the developer's design judgment, because passing and failing signals no longer clearly reflect the code under test.

questions.question: What fixture scope is the safest default for strong independence?
questions.answer: Function scope, because each test gets its own fresh environment unless there is a good reason to broaden sharing.

questions.question: What does an outer transaction buy you in a database-backed test?
questions.answer: It allows realistic persistence behavior, including commits, while still giving you a reliable rollback boundary after the test finishes.

questions.question: Why do async tests typically still run sequentially in the course's model?

options.A: Because async syntax is discouraged
options.B: Because reliability and isolation matter more than hidden runner concurrency
options.C: Because pytest cannot run async tests

questions.explanation: Sequential reliability is preferred over surprising loop interactions.

questions.question: What is the better advanced testing rule?

options.A: Mock every boundary
options.B: Use realistic boundaries but control lifecycle aggressively
options.C: Reuse one global event loop always

questions.explanation: The course favors realism where it teaches you something and strong lifecycle control around it.

M03.title: Choose the Right Python Testing Surface - Quick Check

questions.question: Why is pytest the default tool recommendation in this course?
questions.answer: Because it reduces friction in the TDD loop with lightweight test syntax, fixtures, parametrization, and clearer failure output.

questions.question: What does pytest often improve before a codebase is structurally migrated?
questions.answer: Execution ergonomics such as selection, tracebacks, and output clarity, even when older unittest tests still remain in place.

questions.question: What does 'migrate by pressure' mean?
questions.answer: Rewrite toward pytest-native style where it materially reduces testing friction, not because the team wants a broad rewrite for its own sake.

questions.question: Which feature does not come automatically inside `unittest.TestCase` methods just because pytest runs them?

options.A: Fixture argument injection
options.B: Tracebacks
options.C: Test selection

questions.explanation: Architectural differences still block full pytest fixture arguments inside `TestCase` methods.

questions.question: What is the best first question when choosing a test surface?

options.A: Which tool is trendiest?
options.B: Which surface makes the next useful test easiest to express?
options.C: Which tool uses the fewest imports?

questions.explanation: The course evaluates tools by how they support practical short-cycle testing work.

M04.title: Design Seams the Tests Can Trust - Quick Check

questions.question: What problem does an owned seam solve?
questions.answer: It stops business logic and tests from depending directly on volatile third-party detail by giving the application its own collaborator interface.

questions.question: Why is dependency injection useful at a boundary?
questions.answer: It lets the code receive a collaborator cleanly so tests can control it without deep patching or construction tricks.

questions.question: What still needs verification after adding a seam?
questions.answer: The adapter or translation layer still needs a realistic check to prove it speaks correctly to the real boundary.

questions.question: Where is a seam often best placed?

options.A: At random so more classes exist
options.B: Where foreign detail becomes domain intent
options.C: Only at the UI layer

questions.explanation: The course emphasizes seams at the boundary where business meaning emerges from external detail.

questions.question: Which statement best matches the course?

options.A: Seams remove the need for integration coverage
options.B: Seams improve unit clarity but do not erase boundary verification needs
options.C: Seams matter only in Java-like languages
options.D: Seams are just another name for mocks

questions.explanation: The course separates domain clarity from adapter realism rather than pretending one replaces the other.

M05.title: Use Mocks, Fakes, and Contracts Intentionally - Quick Check

questions.question: What makes a mock helpful instead of misleading in this course?
questions.answer: It should be narrow, aimed at an owned collaborator, and constrained enough to resemble the real interface rather than accepting anything.

questions.question: What is the rule for patch location?
questions.answer: Patch where the code under test looks the object up, not merely where that object was originally defined.

questions.question: Why might a verified fake beat a deep mock for an external API?
questions.answer: Because it stays fast while preserving more truth about the service behavior your application actually depends on.

questions.question: What is the course's best selection heuristic?

options.A: Always prefer mocks
options.B: Pick the cheapest test that still tells the truth about the boundary
options.C: Always prefer end-to-end tests
options.D: Prefer whichever tool has fewer lines

questions.explanation: The course chooses between test forms by truthfulness and cost, not slogans.

questions.question: When are contract-style checks most useful?

options.A: When independently evolving services need explicit interaction verification
options.B: Only for pure functions
options.C: Only in frontend unit tests

questions.explanation: Contract-style checks are most useful where consumer and provider expectations need explicit protection.

M06.title: Broaden Specification Beyond Single Examples - Quick Check

questions.question: When is parametrization the better move than writing several separate tests?
questions.answer: When multiple concrete cases express the same behavior pattern and can be captured with one shared test structure.

questions.question: What does Hypothesis give you beyond hand-written examples?
questions.answer: It checks a property across generated inputs and can surface a falsifying example you would not likely have written by hand.

questions.question: What should determine whether you add a new test layer?
questions.answer: Whether that layer answers a distinct question about logic, invariants, lifecycle, or boundary correctness.

questions.question: What is the course's warning about advanced coverage?

options.A: Never use generated tests
options.B: Do not add every test type by habit
options.C: Avoid persistence realism

questions.explanation: The module rejects piling on test types without a clear learning job for each one.

questions.question: What kind of statement does a property-based test usually express?

options.A: A class name
options.B: An invariant
options.C: A fixture scope

questions.explanation: Properties state invariants that should hold across many inputs.

M07.title: Adopt TDD Without Overclaiming It - Quick Check

questions.question: What is the safest summary of the empirical TDD evidence in this course?
questions.answer: The evidence is mixed and context-sensitive, so teams should validate outcomes locally rather than treating TDD as universally proven or useless.

questions.question: Why is mixed evidence not a reason to dismiss TDD outright?
questions.answer: Because mixed findings still allow meaningful benefits in some contexts; they just do not justify universal promises.

questions.question: What should a team do with open limits like ambiguous contract terminology or large-system uncertainty?
questions.answer: Turn them into explicit operating constraints and validation tasks instead of hiding them behind confident language.

questions.question: Which move best reflects the course's evidence stance?

options.A: Promise TDD will improve everything
options.B: Reject TDD because studies are mixed
options.C: Adopt carefully and measure local results
options.D: Ignore research and trust anecdotes only

questions.explanation: The course closes by recommending local validation instead of sweeping claims.

questions.question: What should happen before a team says it is doing contract testing?

options.A: Nothing, the term is self-explanatory
options.B: The team should define the exact mechanism it means
options.C: It should delete all mocks

questions.explanation: The course treats precise mechanism naming as part of honest technical practice.

M01.title: Practical applications — Drive Design with Red-Green-Refactor

applications.title: Build a Tiny String Calculator Kata
applications.summary: Turn red-green-refactor into a concrete habit by shipping a tiny calculator that grows through deliberately small tests.
applications.prompt: Create a small Python package called `string_calculator` that starts with one passing case, then grows through comma-separated inputs, newlines, and invalid-input behavior one failing test at a time.
applications.deliverable: A runnable Python project with a focused README, a passing test suite, and a short log explaining each red-green-refactor step you took.
steps: Start with one failing test for a single number input.
Grow the parser one behavior at a time instead of designing the whole interface upfront.
After each green test, note whether refactoring improved the next likely step.
Write a brief README section describing how the design emerged from the test sequence.
success_criteria: The project history or notes show multiple small TDD cycles rather than one large implementation drop.
The README explains at least one refactor that made the next test easier to express.
The suite stays readable and fast enough to rerun continuously.
extension_ideas: Add custom delimiter support without rewriting earlier tests.
Record a short screencast narrating your red-green-refactor decisions.

M02.title: Practical applications — Control State, Isolation, and Cleanup

applications.title: Harden a File-Backed Test Harness
applications.summary: Practice isolation by building a tiny app that writes files and proving your tests leave no residue behind.
applications.prompt: Build a command-line note saver that writes and reads JSON notes from disk, then design its tests so every case controls setup and teardown explicitly.
applications.deliverable: A small CLI project plus a test harness that uses temp directories or fixtures to guarantee clean state across runs.
steps: Write one end-to-end test that saves and reloads a note using an isolated temp path.
Add failure-path tests for malformed files or missing storage.
Make cleanup deterministic so rerunning the suite in any order still passes.
Document which parts you kept realistic and which parts you isolated.
success_criteria: Tests never depend on manually cleaned folders or pre-existing files.
Async or persistence boundaries are handled intentionally rather than implicitly.
The suite can run repeatedly without flaky state leakage.
extension_ideas: Swap disk storage for SQLite behind a seam and keep the tests trustworthy.
Add an async save path and document how you controlled event-loop scope.

M03.title: Practical applications — Choose the Right Python Testing Surface

applications.title: Migrate a Legacy unittest Slice to pytest
applications.summary: Use the framework tradeoffs in a practical migration rather than arguing about them abstractly.
applications.prompt: Take a small `unittest`-style module or a toy legacy test file and migrate only the parts where pytest materially improves readability, fixtures, or parametrization.
applications.deliverable: A before-and-after repository slice showing what stayed in unittest, what moved to pytest, and why.
steps: Run the legacy tests under pytest first without rewriting them.
Identify one repeated pattern where fixtures or parametrization reduce friction.
Migrate only that slice and compare clarity, not just syntax.
Write a migration note explaining what remained in place and why.
success_criteria: The migration is pressure-driven rather than total-rewrite theater.
You can point to one concrete ergonomics gain from the pytest-native slice.
Compatibility constraints are documented honestly.
extension_ideas: Add a parametrized edge-case matrix for one behavior.
Turn your migration note into an internal engineering guideline.

M04.title: Practical applications — Design Seams the Tests Can Trust

applications.title: Wrap a Third-Party API Behind an Owned Seam
applications.summary: Design a small project around an adapter so your tests target behavior instead of vendor call shapes.
applications.prompt: Build a tiny weather, email, or currency-conversion service wrapper with an owned interface and tests that exercise your business logic without coupling directly to the external client.
applications.deliverable: A small service module with an adapter, business logic tests, and a note showing what contract the seam protects.
steps: Define the business-facing interface before choosing how the external call is made.
Write tests against the owned seam rather than the vendor client API.
Keep one realistic boundary test so you do not lose external behavior entirely.
Document how the seam would let you replace the provider later.
success_criteria: Business rules are testable without importing third-party response shapes everywhere.
At least one realistic boundary check remains in the suite.
The seam makes provider replacement or persistence changes cheaper.
extension_ideas: Add retry or fallback behavior behind the same seam.
Introduce a second adapter implementation and keep the calling code unchanged.

M05.title: Practical applications — Use Mocks, Fakes, and Contracts Intentionally

applications.title: Replace Brittle Deep Mocks with a Verified Fake
applications.summary: Build a more trustworthy collaborator strategy by comparing a strict mock approach against a fake with real behavior.
applications.prompt: Create a small notification or payment workflow where you first test with strict mocks, then replace the deeper infrastructure simulation with a verified fake and compare the results.
applications.deliverable: A repository slice containing both approaches, plus a short comparison of confidence, brittleness, and maintenance cost.
steps: Start with strict mocks around an owned collaborator.
Notice where the mocks begin to model infrastructure rather than business intent.
Introduce a verified fake or contract-style check for the deeper boundary.
Write up which parts stayed mocked and which parts moved to higher-fidelity verification.
success_criteria: Mocks remain narrow and intentional rather than sprawling across system boundaries.
The verified fake or contract check demonstrates behavior a deep mock could miss.
You can explain the tradeoff without resorting to blanket anti-mock slogans.
extension_ideas: Add autospec and patch-location mistakes intentionally, then document the failure modes.
Turn the comparison into a team decision rubric.

M06.title: Practical applications — Broaden Specification Beyond Single Examples

applications.title: Broaden a Real Parser with Parametrized and Property Tests
applications.summary: Apply advanced specification techniques by hardening a small but realistic parser or validator.
applications.prompt: Choose a small parser, formatter, or validator function and expand its coverage with parametrized examples plus at least one property-based invariant.
applications.deliverable: A mini project where one real behavior is covered by both concrete case tables and generated invariants, with notes on what each style revealed.
tools: hypothesis or equivalent property testing tool
steps: Start from a function that already has a few concrete example tests.
Refactor repeated cases into parametrized tests.
Add at least one generated property that expresses an invariant or round-trip behavior.
Record one surprising falsifying example and what it taught you.
success_criteria: Concrete examples and generated tests complement each other rather than duplicate effort.
A property expresses a real invariant, not just random inputs for spectacle.
The test suite still communicates intent clearly.
extension_ideas: Add an async or persistence boundary and decide which broader technique still fits.
Publish the parser as a small utility package.

M07.title: Practical applications — Adopt TDD Without Overclaiming It

applications.title: Write a TDD Adoption Plan for a Real Team
applications.summary: Turn the evidence limits into an adoption artifact instead of a generic opinion about TDD.
applications.prompt: Draft a concrete adoption plan for one real team or codebase, covering where TDD is likely to help, where evidence is thin, and what outcomes you would measure locally.
applications.deliverable: A short project brief or internal memo with scope, pilot area, risks, metrics, and rollback criteria.
steps: Choose a real team, repo, or problem area rather than talking in abstractions.
State which TDD outcome claims you are willing to rely on and which you are not.
Define a narrow pilot, success metrics, and conditions that would invalidate the approach.
Name the gaps you would still need to validate inside the target environment.
success_criteria: The plan distinguishes evidence-backed expectations from optimistic assumptions.
Metrics are concrete enough to inspect after a pilot.
The rollout scope is small enough to learn from without organizational theater.
extension_ideas: Convert the memo into a lightweight pilot charter template for future teams.
Pair it with one of the earlier module projects as the pilot workload.

paths.title: Complete Course
paths.description: All 14 lessons in the recommended order, from canonical red-green-refactor through empirical limits and open gaps.

paths.title: Practical Builder Path
paths.description: Focus on the daily engineering decisions that make Python TDD usable in real codebases with persistence and external boundaries.

paths.title: Integration and Boundary Path
paths.description: Prioritize isolation, boundary realism, seams, mocks, fakes, contracts, and broader coverage for service-heavy Python systems.

paths.title: Evidence-Aware Adoption Path
paths.description: Move from the canonical loop to adoption strategy with explicit attention to what the literature supports and where it remains thin.
